title: Multiple Sclerosis
oneLiner: Chronic autoimmune demyelinating CNS disease; young adults; relapsing or progressive; early treatment reduces disability.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal cord compression (e.g., epidural abscess, tumour):** bilateral leg weakness + sensory level + back pain + bladder retention — requires urgent MRI spine and neurosurgery review, not steroids alone.
- **CNS infection (meningoencephalitis, HSV, TB):** fever + headache + altered GCS or new neurology — do lumbar puncture before steroids; CSF pleocytosis and low glucose point to infection, not demyelination.
- **Neuromyelitis optica (NMO) relapse:** rapid severe optic neuritis + longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM) — anti-AQP4 serology; treatment is high-dose steroids and plasma exchange, not standard MS DMT.
sections.Presentation:
- **Optic neuritis:** unilateral pain on eye movement, blurred vision, reduced colour saturation, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD)
- **Transverse myelitis:** bilateral limb weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction (urinary retention, constipation)
- **Brainstem/cerebellar:** diplopia, nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria, vertigo
- **Other:** Lhermitte’s sign (electric shock down spine on neck flexion), trigeminal neuralgia, fatigue, Uhthoff’s phenomenon (worsening with heat)
- **Exam:** UMN signs (hypertonia, hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantars), reduced vibration/proprioception, Romberg positive, scanning speech
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Neuromyelitis optica (NMO):** severe optic neuritis + LETM; anti-AQP4 antibody positive (splitter: no brain oligoclonal bands, but positive serum)
- **CNS vasculitis:** headache, encephalopathy, multifocal MRI lesions; labs (ANCA, ESR/CRP), brain biopsy needed
- **Neurosarcoidosis:** pulmonary involvement, hilar lymphadenopathy, chronic meningitis; CSF ACE, MRI meningeal enhancement
- **Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM):** monophasic, post-infectious, widespread MRI lesions, usually children/young adults
- **Functional neurological disorder:** inconsistent weakness, Hoover’s sign, non-anatomical sensory loss; normal MRI and LP
- **Vitamin B12 deficiency:** subacute combined degeneration; check B12, homocysteine, methylmalonic acid; MCV may be high

**Splitter:** Optic neuritis with severe vision loss (<6/60) and longitudinally extensive cord lesion (≥3 vertebral segments) → NMO, not MS.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** visual acuity and colour vision (Ishihara plates), fundoscopy (optic disc swelling in acute optic neuritis), bladder scan for retention, neurological exam including sensory level
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, ESR, CRP, autoimmune screen (ANA, ANCA, anti-dsDNA), anti-AQP4 (NMO), vitamin B12, folate, thyroid function, Lyme serology if tick exposure, HIV, syphilis
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain + spinal cord (cervical and thoracic) with gadolinium — look for periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal lesions; active lesions enhance
  - **Second-line:** MRI orbits if optic neuritis not clinically clear; CT head only if MRI contraindicated
- **Special tests:** Lumbar puncture — measure opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands (OCBs); unmatched CSF-specific OCBs are key for MS diagnosis (vs serum)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** 2017 McDonald criteria — requires demonstration of dissemination in space (≥2 of 4 typical CNS areas) and time (new lesion on follow-up MRI or simultaneous asymptomatic + symptomatic enhancing lesions)
- **Never-miss test:** **Lumbar puncture with OCBs** if MRI is equivocal or atypical — missing it delays accurate diagnosis and risks inappropriate steroids if infection is present
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude mimics: check vital signs, GCS, neck stiffness, fundoscopy
  - If suspected acute relapse with significant disability (e.g., unable to walk, severe vision loss) → notify neurology team
  - Do not start steroids until infection ruled out (CSF result ideally, but if high suspicion of MS and no fever/meningism, can proceed after negative LP)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Acute relapse:** IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 5 days (check local protocol for oral high-dose alternative)
  - Monitor for steroid side-effects: hyperglycaemia, insomnia, mood changes, hypertension
  - If steroid-resistant or severe (e.g., transverse myelitis with complete paraplegia) → consider plasma exchange (5-7 sessions)
  - Bladder care: catheterise if urinary retention (do not use long-term without discussion with urology)
  - Falls risk assessment, physiotherapy and occupational therapy referral early
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to neurology specialist MS clinic for disease-modifying therapy (DMT) discussion: fingolimod, dimethyl fumarate, ocrelizumab, cladribine, teriflunomide, etc.
  - Symptom management: gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain, baclofen/tizanidine for spasticity, amantadine or modafinil for fatigue
  - Lifestyle advice: avoid overheating, balanced diet, vitamin D supplementation (NICE recommends 800-2000 IU/day)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/hour (adjust if comorbidities)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (unless contraindicated or mobilising)
  - Diet: normal unless swallowing assessment needed (dysphagia in brainstem relapse)
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (acute relapse):** Methylprednisolone 1g IV in 250mL 0.9% sodium chloride over 1-2 hours, once daily for 5 days. Alternative: oral methylprednisolone 1g daily for 5 days **or** oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day tapering — [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives (steroid-resistant):** Plasma exchange — volume and frequency per local protocol; usually 5-7 sessions over 2 weeks
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Steroids — avoid live vaccines, monitor blood glucose, use with caution in severe hypertension, active peptic ulcer, systemic infection
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Methylprednisolone dose adjustment not usually required; but caution in severe hepatic impairment (consider shorter course)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving high-dose IV methylprednisolone without checking for active infection (especially HSV, TB, fungal)** — can cause disseminated infection; also, **not checking glucose levels in known diabetics** (can precipitate DKA or hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar state)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pseudorelapse (exacerbation of old symptoms due to infection, fever, stress) — do not treat with steroids; treat underlying infection
  - Steroid-induced: hyperglycaemia, psychosis, pancreatitis, avascular necrosis (rare with short course)
  - Urinary retention → UTI, urosepsis; retention can be painless (check bladder volume)
- **Chronic:**
  - Secondary progressive MS (most relapsing-remitting patients transition after ~15-20 years)
  - Falls, fractures, pressure sores from immobility
  - Chronic pain, spasticity, bladder/bowel dysfunction, depression, cognitive impairment
  - Osteoporosis (steroid use, immobility)
sections.Prognosis: - Median time from onset to needing walking aid is 25-30 years; early DMT improves outcomes. Life expectancy reduced by ~5-10 years (but improving). Relapsing-remitting has better prognosis than primary progressive.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check for UTI or other infection** before attributing new neurology to a relapse — a positive urinalysis + pyuria may indicate pseudorelapse.
- **Assess visual acuity and colour vision** in all suspected optic neuritis — this is a quantifiable baseline and essential for monitoring response.
- **Document neurological exam carefully** including sensory level, power (MRC scale), and bladder scan — this guides steroid response assessment.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting steroids before CSF or MRI to rule out infection** in atypical presentations (e.g., fever, altered GCS, immunocompromised) — can cause harm.
- **Assuming all white matter lesions on MRI are MS** — consider migraine, microvascular disease, vasculitis, NMO; get neuroradiology review if uncertain.
- **Using DMTs without excluding progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)** in patients on natalizumab or fingolimod — check JC virus antibody status first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New blurred vision, loss of colour vision, limb weakness, difficulty walking, urinary retention, or worsening after initial improvement.
- **Follow-up:** Neurology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks; urgent ophthalmology if optic neuritis not improving after 1 week of steroids.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neurology registrar if:** NEWS2 ≥5, GCS drop, new spinal cord signs (sensory level, complete paraplegia suggesting NMO or cord compression), failure to improve after 3 days of steroids (consider plasma exchange)
- **ICU / crash team:** any sign of airway compromise (brainstem relapse: respiratory depression, aspiration), status epilepticus (rare), or anaphylaxis to steroids/immunosuppressants

rawMarkdown:
## Multiple Sclerosis
**One-liner:** Chronic autoimmune demyelinating CNS disease; young adults; relapsing or progressive; early treatment reduces disability.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal cord compression (e.g., epidural abscess, tumour):** bilateral leg weakness + sensory level + back pain + bladder retention — requires urgent MRI spine and neurosurgery review, not steroids alone.
- **CNS infection (meningoencephalitis, HSV, TB):** fever + headache + altered GCS or new neurology — do lumbar puncture before steroids; CSF pleocytosis and low glucose point to infection, not demyelination.
- **Neuromyelitis optica (NMO) relapse:** rapid severe optic neuritis + longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM) — anti-AQP4 serology; treatment is high-dose steroids and plasma exchange, not standard MS DMT.

### Presentation
- **Optic neuritis:** unilateral pain on eye movement, blurred vision, reduced colour saturation, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD)
- **Transverse myelitis:** bilateral limb weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction (urinary retention, constipation)
- **Brainstem/cerebellar:** diplopia, nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria, vertigo
- **Other:** Lhermitte’s sign (electric shock down spine on neck flexion), trigeminal neuralgia, fatigue, Uhthoff’s phenomenon (worsening with heat)
- **Exam:** UMN signs (hypertonia, hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantars), reduced vibration/proprioception, Romberg positive, scanning speech

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Neuromyelitis optica (NMO):** severe optic neuritis + LETM; anti-AQP4 antibody positive (splitter: no brain oligoclonal bands, but positive serum)
- **CNS vasculitis:** headache, encephalopathy, multifocal MRI lesions; labs (ANCA, ESR/CRP), brain biopsy needed
- **Neurosarcoidosis:** pulmonary involvement, hilar lymphadenopathy, chronic meningitis; CSF ACE, MRI meningeal enhancement
- **Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM):** monophasic, post-infectious, widespread MRI lesions, usually children/young adults
- **Functional neurological disorder:** inconsistent weakness, Hoover’s sign, non-anatomical sensory loss; normal MRI and LP
- **Vitamin B12 deficiency:** subacute combined degeneration; check B12, homocysteine, methylmalonic acid; MCV may be high

**Splitter:** Optic neuritis with severe vision loss (<6/60) and longitudinally extensive cord lesion (≥3 vertebral segments) → NMO, not MS.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** visual acuity and colour vision (Ishihara plates), fundoscopy (optic disc swelling in acute optic neuritis), bladder scan for retention, neurological exam including sensory level
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, ESR, CRP, autoimmune screen (ANA, ANCA, anti-dsDNA), anti-AQP4 (NMO), vitamin B12, folate, thyroid function, Lyme serology if tick exposure, HIV, syphilis
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain + spinal cord (cervical and thoracic) with gadolinium — look for periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal lesions; active lesions enhance
  - **Second-line:** MRI orbits if optic neuritis not clinically clear; CT head only if MRI contraindicated
- **Special tests:** Lumbar puncture — measure opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands (OCBs); unmatched CSF-specific OCBs are key for MS diagnosis (vs serum)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** 2017 McDonald criteria — requires demonstration of dissemination in space (≥2 of 4 typical CNS areas) and time (new lesion on follow-up MRI or simultaneous asymptomatic + symptomatic enhancing lesions)
- **Never-miss test:** **Lumbar puncture with OCBs** if MRI is equivocal or atypical — missing it delays accurate diagnosis and risks inappropriate steroids if infection is present

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude mimics: check vital signs, GCS, neck stiffness, fundoscopy
  - If suspected acute relapse with significant disability (e.g., unable to walk, severe vision loss) → notify neurology team
  - Do not start steroids until infection ruled out (CSF result ideally, but if high suspicion of MS and no fever/meningism, can proceed after negative LP)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Acute relapse:** IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 5 days (check local protocol for oral high-dose alternative)
  - Monitor for steroid side-effects: hyperglycaemia, insomnia, mood changes, hypertension
  - If steroid-resistant or severe (e.g., transverse myelitis with complete paraplegia) → consider plasma exchange (5-7 sessions)
  - Bladder care: catheterise if urinary retention (do not use long-term without discussion with urology)
  - Falls risk assessment, physiotherapy and occupational therapy referral early
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to neurology specialist MS clinic for disease-modifying therapy (DMT) discussion: fingolimod, dimethyl fumarate, ocrelizumab, cladribine, teriflunomide, etc.
  - Symptom management: gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain, baclofen/tizanidine for spasticity, amantadine or modafinil for fatigue
  - Lifestyle advice: avoid overheating, balanced diet, vitamin D supplementation (NICE recommends 800-2000 IU/day)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/hour (adjust if comorbidities)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (unless contraindicated or mobilising)
  - Diet: normal unless swallowing assessment needed (dysphagia in brainstem relapse)
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (acute relapse):** Methylprednisolone 1g IV in 250mL 0.9% sodium chloride over 1-2 hours, once daily for 5 days. Alternative: oral methylprednisolone 1g daily for 5 days **or** oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day tapering — [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives (steroid-resistant):** Plasma exchange — volume and frequency per local protocol; usually 5-7 sessions over 2 weeks
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Steroids — avoid live vaccines, monitor blood glucose, use with caution in severe hypertension, active peptic ulcer, systemic infection
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Methylprednisolone dose adjustment not usually required; but caution in severe hepatic impairment (consider shorter course)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving high-dose IV methylprednisolone without checking for active infection (especially HSV, TB, fungal)** — can cause disseminated infection; also, **not checking glucose levels in known diabetics** (can precipitate DKA or hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar state)

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pseudorelapse (exacerbation of old symptoms due to infection, fever, stress) — do not treat with steroids; treat underlying infection
  - Steroid-induced: hyperglycaemia, psychosis, pancreatitis, avascular necrosis (rare with short course)
  - Urinary retention → UTI, urosepsis; retention can be painless (check bladder volume)
- **Chronic:**
  - Secondary progressive MS (most relapsing-remitting patients transition after ~15-20 years)
  - Falls, fractures, pressure sores from immobility
  - Chronic pain, spasticity, bladder/bowel dysfunction, depression, cognitive impairment
  - Osteoporosis (steroid use, immobility)

### Prognosis
- Median time from onset to needing walking aid is 25-30 years; early DMT improves outcomes. Life expectancy reduced by ~5-10 years (but improving). Relapsing-remitting has better prognosis than primary progressive.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check for UTI or other infection** before attributing new neurology to a relapse — a positive urinalysis + pyuria may indicate pseudorelapse.
- **Assess visual acuity and colour vision** in all suspected optic neuritis — this is a quantifiable baseline and essential for monitoring response.
- **Document neurological exam carefully** including sensory level, power (MRC scale), and bladder scan — this guides steroid response assessment.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting steroids before CSF or MRI to rule out infection** in atypical presentations (e.g., fever, altered GCS, immunocompromised) — can cause harm.
- **Assuming all white matter lesions on MRI are MS** — consider migraine, microvascular disease, vasculitis, NMO; get neuroradiology review if uncertain.
- **Using DMTs without excluding progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)** in patients on natalizumab or fingolimod — check JC virus antibody status first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New blurred vision, loss of colour vision, limb weakness, difficulty walking, urinary retention, or worsening after initial improvement.
- **Follow-up:** Neurology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks; urgent ophthalmology if optic neuritis not improving after 1 week of steroids.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neurology registrar if:** NEWS2 ≥5, GCS drop, new spinal cord signs (sensory level, complete paraplegia suggesting NMO or cord compression), failure to improve after 3 days of steroids (consider plasma exchange)
- **ICU / crash team:** any sign of airway compromise (brainstem relapse: respiratory depression, aspiration), status epilepticus (rare), or anaphylaxis to steroids/immunosuppressants
